exercises.title: String length
hints: Strings use .length() with parens (method)
Count every character INCLUDING the space
H-e-l-l-o-(space)-W-o-r-l-d = 11 characters

exercises.title: Immutability check
exercises.scenario: Strings are immutable — predict what happens.
hints: Strings are IMMUTABLE
toUpperCase() returns a NEW string, original unchanged
We didn't capture the return value, so it's discarded
s still points to 'hello'
To change: s = s.toUpperCase()

exercises.title: Convert and capture
exercises.scenario: Fix the immutability mistake by capturing the result.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Convert` that:
1. Has String s = "hello world"
2. Converts it to uppercase AND captures the result
3. Prints the uppercase version

Expected output:
HELLO WORLD
hints: Don't just call s.toUpperCase() — capture it
Assign back: s = s.toUpperCase()
Or use a new variable: String upper = s.toUpperCase()

exercises.title: Fix the == bug
exercises.instructions:
This code SHOULD print 'Premium access granted!' but doesn't. Fix it.

Expected output:
Premium access granted!

bugs.issue: == compares references, not content. The String from getUserPlan() is a different object than the literal "premium".

hints: Never use == to compare Strings
Use .equals() instead: plan.equals("premium")
The 'premium' from getUserPlan is a different object than the literal
This bug causes real production incidents

exercises.title: Trim and lowercase user input
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `CleanInput` that:
1. Has String userEmail = "  AISHA@Gmail.COM  "
2. Cleans it: trim whitespace, then lowercase
3. Print the cleaned version

Expected output:
Cleaned: aisha@gmail.com
hints: Chain method calls: userEmail.trim().toLowerCase()
.trim() removes leading/trailing whitespace
.toLowerCase() converts to lowercase
Each method returns a new String, so we can chain

exercises.title: Character at position
exercises.scenario: Use charAt to access individual characters.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Initials` that:
1. Has firstName = "Aisha" and lastName = "Khan"
2. Gets first character of each (charAt(0))
3. Prints initials like 'A.K.'

Expected output:
Initials: A.K.
hints: charAt(0) returns the FIRST character
Returns a char (primitive), not String
Concatenate: firstInitial + "." + lastInitial + "."

exercises.title: Count vowels
exercises.scenario: Classic interview problem — count vowels in a string.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `VowelCount`:
1. Method `countVowels(String s)` returns count of vowels (a, e, i, o, u — case insensitive)
2. main tests with "Hello World" — should return 3

Expected output:
Vowels in 'Hello World': 3
hints: Convert to lowercase first to simplify checking
Iterate with charAt and index
Check if char is a, e, i, o, u using ==
Vowels in 'Hello World': e, o, o = 3

exercises.title: Substring extraction
exercises.scenario: Extract a portion of a string.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Extract`:
1. Has String url = "https://github.com/anthropic"
2. Extract just the domain (between '//' and the next '/')
3. Print 'Domain: <domain>'

For this URL, domain should be 'github.com'.

Expected output:
Domain: github.com
hints: Find '//' position with indexOf
Domain starts AFTER '//', so add 2 to indexOf position
Find next '/' using indexOf with fromIndex parameter
substring(start, end) — end is EXCLUSIVE
For our URL: '//' at 6, next '/' at 16. Domain = substring(8, 16) = 'github.com'

exercises.title: Split CSV line
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `CSVParse`:
1. Has String line = "Aisha,28,Hyderabad,Engineer"
2. Split by comma, store in array
3. Print each field on its own line

Expected output:
Field 0: Aisha
Field 1: 28
Field 2: Hyderabad
Field 3: Engineer

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Field 0: Aisha
Field 1: 28
Field 2: Hyderabad
Field 3: Engineer

hints: line.split(",") returns String[]
Iterate with index to print 'Field i: value'
split is THE primary parsing tool — used in every CSV reader

exercises.title: Fix the concatenation order
exercises.instructions:
This code SHOULD print 'Total: 30' but prints something else. Fix the concatenation logic.

Expected output:
Total: 30

bugs.issue: Left-to-right: "Total: " + 10 becomes "Total: 10", then "Total: 10" + 20 becomes "Total: 1020". Once + sees a String, it concatenates.
bugs.fix: Wrap a + b in parens to force addition first

hints: Output is currently 'Total: 1020' (concatenation, not addition)
Left-to-right evaluation: "Total: " + 10 first → "Total: 10"
Then "Total: 10" + 20 → "Total: 1020"
Wrap math in parens: (a + b) forces addition first
Result: "Total: " + 30 → "Total: 30"

exercises.title: Reverse a string
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Reverse`:
1. Method `reverse(String s)` returns the reversed string
2. main tests with 'hello' → 'olleh'

Use a loop (don't use StringBuilder.reverse — we want practice).

Expected output:
Reverse of 'hello': olleh
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your Reverse class below
// Use a loop, NOT StringBuilder.reverse()
hints: Iterate from last index to first: i = s.length() - 1; i >= 0; i--
Build result by concatenating each char: result += s.charAt(i)
Note: this is INEFFICIENT for long strings (next topic explains why and how StringBuilder fixes it)
For now, the pattern works for learning

exercises.title: Check string palindrome
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Palindrome`:
1. Method `isPalindrome(String s)` returns true if s reads same forwards and backwards (case-insensitive)
2. main tests:
   - "racecar" → true
   - "hello" → false
   - "Level" → true (case-insensitive)

Use two pointers (left and right), not reverse-and-compare.

Expected output:
'racecar' palindrome? true
'hello' palindrome? false
'Level' palindrome? true
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your Palindrome class below
// Use two-pointer technique, NOT reverse-and-compare

test_cases.expected_stdout:
'racecar' palindrome? true
'hello' palindrome? false
'Level' palindrome? true

hints: Convert to lowercase first for case-insensitive comparison
Two pointers: left = 0, right = s.length() - 1
While left < right: if charAt(left) != charAt(right) → not palindrome
Otherwise move pointers inward
Same two-pointer pattern as Topic 3.2

exercises.title: Count word occurrences
exercises.scenario: Find how many times a word appears in a sentence.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `WordCount`:
1. Method `countOccurrences(String text, String word)` returns count of word in text (case-insensitive)
2. Use split to break text into words
3. main tests with: "The cat sat on the mat" and word "the" → 2

Expected output:
'the' appears 2 times in 'The cat sat on the mat'

test_cases.expected_stdout: 'the' appears 2 times in 'The cat sat on the mat'

hints: Lowercase both text and word for case-insensitive matching
Split text by space: text.split(" ")
Loop through words array, count matches with .equals()
NEVER use == for string comparison
'The' and 'the' in lowercase both become 'the' → count 2

exercises.title: Convert sentence to title case
exercises.scenario: Capitalize the first letter of each word.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `TitleCase`:
1. Method `toTitleCase(String s)` capitalizes first letter of each word, lowercase rest
2. main tests with "hello world from JAVA" → 'Hello World From Java'

Approach: split by space, transform each word, join back.

Expected output:
Title case: Hello World From Java

test_cases.expected_stdout: Title case: Hello World From Java

hints: Split into words by space
For each word: lowercase entire word, then uppercase first char
Capitalize: word.substring(0, 1).toUpperCase() + word.substring(1)
Join back with spaces between words
Note: + in loops is slow due to immutability — fix in next topic with StringBuilder

exercises.title: Find first non-repeating character
exercises.scenario: Classic interview question — find the first character that appears only once.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `FirstUnique`:
1. Method `firstNonRepeating(String s)` returns the first character that appears exactly once in s. If all characters repeat, return ' ' (space) as sentinel.
2. main tests:
   - "swiss" → 'w' (s repeats, w is unique, then i unique, then s repeats — first unique is w)
   - "hello" → 'h' (h unique)
   - "aabbcc" → ' ' (all repeat)

Approach: For each character, count its total occurrences in the string. Return first with count = 1.

Expected output:
First unique in 'swiss': w
First unique in 'hello': h
First unique in 'aabbcc': (space — all repeat)

test_cases.expected_stdout:
First unique in 'swiss': w
First unique in 'hello': h
First unique in 'aabbcc':   (space if all repeat)

hints: Outer loop: each character in string
Inner loop: count how many times that character appears total
If count == 1, this is the first unique — return it
If outer loop finishes with no unique found, return ' ' (space) as sentinel
For 'swiss': s appears 2 times, w appears 1 time → return 'w'
This is O(n²) — could be optimized with HashMap (covered in Collections module)
